sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has an orange background with: - Top green bar (row 0) - likely timer/progress - Charcoal 3x4 + Magenta 4x4 stacked at cols 36-39, rows 1-7 - likely the player avatar - Blue 4x20 horizontal bar at rows 16-19 - a platform/target - Two yellow L-shaped blocks at bottom rows 52-59 - obstacles/platforms - Bottom gray bar (rows 60-63) - floor
- Goal model: Unclear yet. Need to test movement to understand mechanics.
- Plan: Move RIGHT x2 and UP x2 to reach the target area.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check if the blue bar moved after this UP action.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check blue bar position after UP
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation['nodes']

print("BEFORE:")
for node in before_seg:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if node['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  b: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

print("AFTER:")
for node in after_seg:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if node['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  b: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blue bar didn't move at all with UP! But earlier, when I first tested UP (from rows 16-19), it did move to rows 12-15. So what changed?
Wait, let me think about this. When I first tested UP, the blue bar was at rows 16-19, and it moved to rows 12-15. Then I did RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP as a batch. After that batch, the blue bar was at rows 12-15, cols 20-39. So the RIGHT moves worked but the UP moves in the batch didn't.
Now I tried UP again and it still didn't move. So there must be something blocking upward movement now. 
Looking at the board layout:
- The charcoal object is at rows 1-3, cols 36-39
- The magenta object is at rows 4-7, cols 36-39
- The blue bar is at rows 12-15, cols 20-39
The blue bar's right edge is at col 39, which overlaps with the charcoal/magenta columns (36-39). The blue bar spans rows 12-15. If I try to move it UP, it would go to rows 8-11. There's nothing between rows 8-11 in that column range... unless there's something I'm missing.
Wait, actually the blue bar at cols 20-39 and rows 12-15. If it moves up to rows 8-11, cols 20-39, there shouldn't be any obstacle. Unless the orange background is acting as a boundary somehow.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the blue bar can only move horizontally? The first UP test showed it moving from 16-19 to 12-15, but maybe that was a one-time thing or I misread it. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, I did verify earlier that UP moved the blue bar from rows 16-19 to rows 12-15. So UP does work. But now it doesn't. What could have changed?
Wait - maybe the blue bar can only move in one direction at a time, or there's some state change. Or maybe the batch execution had some issue where the UP actions were consumed but didn't produce movement because of some constraint.
Let me check if DOWN works now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test DOWN to see if vertical movement still works
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
